text: 6 Minute English from bbclearningenglish .com Hello, this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: 这是来自bbclearningenglish.com的《六分钟英语》,大家好,欢迎收听BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》。

text: I'm Pippa.
zh: 我是Pippa。

text: And I'm Beth.
zh: 我是Beth。

text: Foraging is the activity of walking in the countryside looking for plants you can eat that grow in the wild.
zh: 采集野生食物(foraging)是指在乡间行走,寻找可以食用的野生植物。

text: That's anything from blackberries and chestnuts to mushrooms and and wild garlic.
zh: 从黑莓、栗子到蘑菇、野生大蒜,什么都有。

text: It's something humans have always done.
zh: 这是人类一直以来都在做的事情。

text: And recently, it's become fashionable among groups of young people.
zh: 最近,它在年轻人中变得流行起来。

text: Pippa, have you ever been foraging?
zh: Pippa,你有采集过野生食物吗?

text: Yes, we used to go and pick blackberries when I was younger, but I haven't really done it as an adult.
zh: 有过,我小时候常去采黑莓,但成年后就没怎么做过了。

text: And I would be a bit nervous to look for mushrooms or things like that.
zh: 至于蘑菇之类的东西,我会有点紧张,不敢去找。

text: What about you?
zh: 你呢?

text: I'm exactly the same.
zh: 我也是一样。

text: If I see a blackberry on a bush, I will eat it.
zh: 如果在灌木丛上看到黑莓,我会吃掉它。

text: But mushrooms, No, too scary.
zh: 但蘑菇,不行,太吓人了。

text: So in this episode, we'll meet two young women from different countries who are passionate about foraging wild food.
zh: 在这一期节目中,我们将认识两位来自不同国家、热衷于采集野生食物的年轻女性。

text: They're both self -taught, having learned to forage by studying the natural world around them.
zh: 她们都是通过研究周围的自然世界自学成才的。

text: And it's important to say that they only eat what they can identify with 100 % certainty, sticking to the rule, if in doubt, leave it out.
zh: 需要说明的是,她们只吃百分之百能辨认出来的食物,坚持“如有疑虑,宁可不吃”的原则。

text: We'll also be learning some useful new words and phrases,
zh: 我们还会学习一些实用的新单词和短语,

text: all of which you'll find on our website bbclearningenglish .com but before that i
zh: 这些内容都可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到,不过在那之前,

text: have a question for you pippa as mentioned when foraging you must know for certain what is safe to eat something
zh: 我有个问题要问你,Pippa,正如前面提到的,采集野生食物时,你必须确定什么是安全可食用的。

text: definitely not safe to eat is the mushroom death cap as the name suggests it's one of the world's deadliest mushrooms and it's common across the British Isles.
zh: 有一种绝对不安全的蘑菇叫“死帽蕈”,顾名思义,它是世界上最致命的蘑菇之一,在不列颠群岛很常见。

text: But what colour is it?
zh: 但它是什么颜色的呢?

text: Are death cap mushrooms A brown, B white or C red?
zh: 死帽蕈是A. 棕色,B. 白色,还是C. 红色?

text: I'm gonna say if it's dangerous maybe it's red.
zh: 我猜,如果它很危险,也许是红色。

text: OK, well we'll find out later in the programme.
zh: 好的,我们稍后在节目中揭晓答案。

text: First let's meet Roshana Gray, a wild food forager living in Cape Point, South Africa.
zh: 首先,让我们认识一下Roshana Gray,她是生活在南非开普角的一位野生食物采集者。

text: Roshana learned how to forage from her mother -in -law as she explains here to BBC World Service Programme The Conversation.
zh: Roshana向BBC世界频道节目《The Conversation》讲述了她是如何从婆婆那里学会采集野生食物的。

text: So she is a horticulturist.
zh: 她婆婆是一位园艺师。

text: She has a deep knowledge of plants.
zh: 她对植物有着深厚的了解。

text: And so I would be kind of scanning the landscape and learning about these new plants or new to me and wondering if you could eat them.
zh: 所以我会四处观察周围的环境,了解这些对我来说新奇的植物,并思考它们是否可以食用。

text: Something that's incredibly important in foraging is to know 110 % if you can eat it.
zh: 在采集野生食物时,一件极其重要的事情是要百分之百确定这东西能不能吃。

text: Positive identification of the plant and so she was my plant person.
zh: 准确辨认植物的身份,而她就是我的“植物顾问”。

text: Roshana's mother -in -law is a horticulturalist, a person who studies or grows plants such as flowers, fruits and vegetables.
zh: Roshana的婆婆是一位园艺师,也就是研究或种植花卉、水果、蔬菜等植物的人。

text: She has a deep knowledge of the wild plants growing in nature.
zh: 她对自然界中生长的野生植物有着深厚的了解。

text: A deep knowledge means a thorough understanding of a subject gained from experience and study, not just knowing the basic facts.
zh: “深厚的了解”是指通过经验和学习获得的对某个主题的透彻理解,而不仅仅是知道基本事实。

text: One skill foragers need is scanning the landscape.
zh: 采集者需要具备的一项技能就是“扫视周围环境”。

text: scanning means searching a wide area with your eyes to find some particular thing
zh: scanning(扫视)意味着用眼睛在广阔区域内搜寻某个特定的东西,

text: in this case edible plants and herbs and of course roshana only eats plants that she can positively identify plants that she knows 110 are safe to eat here
zh: 在这里指的是可食用的植物和草药。当然,Roshana只吃她能准确辨认的植物,也就是她百分百确定安全可食用的植物。

text: the idiom 110 means roshana is completely sure she has no doubt and that's important as the consequences of eating poisonous plants are serious sickness or even death.
zh: 这里的习语“110%”意思是Roshana完全确定,毫无疑问,这一点很重要,因为食用有毒植物的后果可能是严重的疾病,甚至死亡。

text: Our second female forager Emily Smith moved to rural Japan to work on a project cataloging and collecting wild mushrooms.
zh: 我们的第二位女性采集者Emily Smith搬到了日本乡下,参与一个野生蘑菇编目和采集的项目。

text: With around 5 ,000 varieties, 300 of which are edible, mushrooms are an important part of traditional Japanese cooking.
zh: 日本大约有5000种蘑菇,其中300种可食用,蘑菇是日本传统烹饪的重要组成部分。

text: But with names like death cap and a a reputation for being poisonous, mushrooms are what many wild foragers worry about the most.
zh: 但由于像“死帽蕈”这样的名字以及蘑菇有毒的名声,蘑菇是许多野生食物采集者最担心的东西。

text: Emily discussed these worries with BBC World Service programme, The Conversation.
zh: Emily在BBC世界频道节目《The Conversation》中谈到了这些担忧。

text: That stigma is a bit misplaced, I think, because plants can be just as dangerous, if not more dangerous than mushrooms.
zh: 我认为这种污名有点不公平,因为植物可能和蘑菇一样危险,甚至更危险。

text: And I think that mushrooms have been much maligned.
zh: 而且我觉得蘑菇一直被过分诋毁了。

text: I mean, you've got to exercise caution, as Roshana said, in anything that you pick, You need to know what it is before you consume it.
zh: 我是说,正如Roshana所说,不管你采什么,都要小心谨慎,在食用之前你需要知道它到底是什么。

text: If in doubt, throw it out is a very good phrase to say.
zh: “如有疑虑,就扔掉”是一句很好的说法。

text: Emily thinks there's a stigma around mushrooms.
zh: Emily认为人们对蘑菇存在一种污名化的看法。

text: A stigma is a negative belief about something which is not necessarily true or fair.
zh: 污名(stigma)是指对某事物的一种负面看法,而这种看法未必真实或公平。

text: In fact, many plants are just as poisonous as mushrooms.
zh: 事实上,许多植物和蘑菇一样有毒。

text: The main thing is to exercise caution, to act with care and attention so as to avoid possible dangers.
zh: 最重要的是要“谨慎行事”(exercise caution),也就是小心行事,以避免可能的危险。

text: In other words, to be careful.
zh: 换句话说,就是要小心。

text: And the best way of doing that is learning the difference between what's safe to eat and what's not.
zh: 而做到这一点的最好方法,就是学会区分什么是安全可食用的,什么不是。

text: Speaking of which, what was the answer to your question, Beth?
zh: 说到这个,Beth,你那个问题的答案是什么?

text: Ah, I asked you, what colour are death cap mushrooms?
zh: 啊,我问你的是,死帽蕈是什么颜色?

text: You said red, which I also thought was the answer, but actually they are white.
zh: 你说是红色,我原本也以为是这个答案,但实际上它是白色的。

text: Right, it's time to recap the vocabulary we've learnt about foraging, starting with horticulturalist, a person who studies or grows plants.
zh: 好的,现在是时候回顾一下我们学到的关于采集野生食物的词汇了,首先是horticulturalist(园艺师),指研究或种植植物的人。

text: If you have a deep knowledge of a subject, you have a thorough understanding of it gained from experience and study.
zh: 如果你对某个主题有“深厚的了解”(a deep knowledge),那说明你通过经验和学习对它有透彻的理解。

text: Scanning involves searching a wide area with your eyes to find some particular thing.
zh: Scanning(扫视)指的是用眼睛搜索广阔区域以寻找某个特定的东西。

text: If you're 110 % sure about something, you're absolutely certain you have no doubt.
zh: 如果你对某件事“百分之百确定”(110% sure),那就是说你完全确信,毫无疑问。

text: A stigma is an unfair negative belief about something.
zh: Stigma(污名)是指对某事物不公平的负面看法。

text: And finally, to exercise caution is a more formal way of saying to be careful, something you should definitely remember if you go wild foraging yourself.
zh: 最后,exercise caution(谨慎行事)是“小心”(be careful)的一种更正式的说法,如果你自己去野外采集食物,一定要记住这一点。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up.
zh: 我们的六分钟又到了。

text: If you enjoyed this episode, you'll find a quiz and worksheet to practice the vocabulary on our website, bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 如果你喜欢这一期节目,可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到相关的测验和练习题,来巩固所学词汇。

text: See you again soon.
zh: 我们下次再见。

text: Bye for now.
zh: 再见啦。

text: Goodbye.
zh: 再见。
